title: Spinning Wensleydale wool
subtitle: A long-staple lustre fleece spun worsted for a DK to aran-weight yarn with high sheen
excerpt: Wensleydale is a long-stapled lustre breed with a distinctive curl that produces yarn with a natural sheen when spun worsted. Combed or hackled preparation gives the best results. The finished yarn has a smooth, slightly slippery handle suited to outerwear and woven projects.
sourceNotes: Wensleydale breed characteristics and spinning behaviour are well-documented in UK guild literature. The Wensleydale is a rare breed, native to North Yorkshire, listed by the Rare Breeds Survival Trust. Spinning characteristics observation-led from guild notes and Rare Breeds Survival Trust breed information.

glossaryTerms.definition: A spinning preparation and technique in which fibres are combed parallel before spinning, producing a smooth, dense yarn with high twist. Worsted yarn shows fibre lustre well and has low halo.
A natural cluster of fibres in the shorn fleece. Wensleydale staples are long (15 to 30 cm) with a distinctive ringlet curl at the tip.

The natural sheen in long-staple breeds such as Wensleydale, Bluefaced Leicester, and Romney. Lustre is caused by large, flat fibre scales that reflect light along the fibre length, particularly visible in worsted-spun yarn.

content.text: Wensleydale is one of the rare
breeds: long-stapled fibres with large flat scales that produce a natural sheen in the spun yarn. The

length runs from 15 to 30 cm and the fibre has a distinctive ringlet curl. Wensleydale is heavier in micron count than merino (typically 33 to 35 microns) and is not a next-to-skin fibre; it is ideal for outerwear, woven scarves, and bags where sheen and durability matter more than softness. The best preparation is
: combed or hackled to align the long parallel fibres before spinning.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair, scarves, and cuffs back from the drive band, flyer, and bobbin. The flyer turns fast and catches fabric instantly.

items.name: Wensleydale raw locks or combed top, 100 g
Spinning wheel with a low ratio setting (4:1 to 5:1)
Measuring tape for checking wraps per inch
Hand combs, optional, for combing raw locks

content.text: If working from raw locks, comb the locks straight using hand combs or a hackle to align the fibres in parallel rows. The characteristic Wensleydale ringlet curl must be completely straightened before spinning; any remaining curl will produce irregular twist uptake and uneven yarn. If working from commercial top, the combing is done; check the top is drafted evenly before attaching to the wheel.

Set the wheel to a low ratio (4:1 to 5:1 for a DK to aran weight yarn). Wensleydale's long staple means it needs more twist than merino to bind properly; the low ratio delivers twist at a manageable rate. Too high a ratio with long-staple fibres causes over-twist in the yarn as it forms.

Use a forward short-draw: hold the fibre supply at the back hand and draft a short section forward with the front hand, allowing the twist to enter only after the draft zone is established. Keep the front pinch tight until the drafted length is the thickness you want; then release to allow twist in. The long fibres slide smoothly against each other and the draft should be even and consistent. Aim for approximately 20 to 22 wraps per 2.5 cm for a DK-weight single.
Step 4: Ply and set the twist

Ply two singles together in the opposite direction (S-ply if the singles were Z-spun). Wensleydale's lustre shows at its best in a 2-ply yarn: the smooth plied surface reflects light along its length. Wind off onto the niddy-noddy into a skein, tie loosely at four points, and soak in warm water for 10 minutes. Snap the skein gently once while wet to set the twist, then hang to dry with a small weight (a 100 g stone hung from the skein loop is enough).

items.problem: The yarn is lively and corkscrews back on itself when released
items.cause: Over-twist in the singles; the ply twist did not fully balance the singles twist
items.fix: Add more ply twist in the opposite direction. Wensleydale takes more ply twist to balance than shorter-staple fibres because the long fibres hold singles twist stubbornly.

items.problem: The fibre breaks in the draft zone repeatedly
items.cause: The combing was incomplete and fibres are crossed rather than parallel
items.fix: Re-comb the preparation. Wensleydale must be fully straightened before spinning; crossed fibres break under draft tension rather than sliding.
